item.title: Continuity
item.description: A function $f$ is continuous at a point $a \in \mathbb{R}$ if the limit of $f$ at $x = a$ exists and is equal to the function value at $a$, $f(a)$.

item.title: Euler's Formula
item.description: Euler’s formula states that for any real number $x$, $$e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x.$$ When $x = \pi$, Euler’s formula can be written in the form $e^{\pi i} + 1 = 0$, which is known as Euler’s identity.

item.title: Function Composition
item.description: Let $f: X \to Y$ and $g: Y \to Z$ be two functions. The composition of $f$ and $g$, denoted $f \circ g: X \to Z$, is defined by

item.title: Functions
item.description: A function $f$ from a set $X$ to a set $Y$, denoted $f: X \to Y$, assigns each element $x \in X$ exactly one element $f(x) \in Y$.

item.title: Injective Function
item.description: A functions$f: X \to Y$ is said to be injective or one-to-one if, for all $x_1, x_2 \in X$, we have $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 = x_2$.

item.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
item.description: Let $f$ be a continuous function defined on $[a, b]$, and $c$ some number such that $f(a) < c < f(b)$.

item.title: Inverse Functions
item.description: Let $f: X \to Y$ be a bijective ( injective function and surjective) function. The inverse function $f^{-1}: Y \to X$ is defined by $f^{-1}(y) = x$ for each $y \in Y$ and $x \in X$.

item.title: Limits
item.description: Limit of a Sequence Let ${a_n}_{n=0}^\infty$ be a sequence. If $a_n$ approaches some number $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$ as $n$ gets larger and larger, we write

item.title: Sequences
item.description: A sequence ${a_n}$ is an ordered list of numbers. Formally, a sequence can be defined as a [[notes/MATH1051/functions|function]] from the natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ to the elements at each point in the sequence.

item.title: Squeeze Theorem
item.description: Squeeze Theorem (sequences) Let $a_n, b_n, c_n$ be sequences such that, for some $N \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $a_n \leq b_n \leq c_n$ for all $n \geq N$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} c_n = \mathscr{l}$.
